completeness.rationale: Six of seven expected extracts are present: task count (~5,000), title-vs-description performance split, v2.2-to-v2.3 regression, 2-week timeline, 10-15 second latency, and long descriptions. The one notable gap is CPU spike behavior during searches — the agent never asked about system resource usage, which would have strengthened the diagnosis and helped developers reproduce the bottleneck.

accuracy.rationale: The root cause hypothesis — that v2.3 dropped or broke a full-text index on description fields, leaving title search on the old indexed path — directly matches both acceptable diagnostic paths and closely approximates the actual cause (FTS5 index not migrated, replaced by a naive LIKE query). All stated facts align with ground truth. The agent correctly identified the version upgrade as the trigger and the title/description split as the key diagnostic signal.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent resolved after two turns once the title-vs-description pattern emerged, which was a reasonable stopping point. However, it left obvious follow-ups on the table: CPU/resource behavior during slow searches (would confirm a scan-heavy operation), exact OS/hardware details (present in ground truth but absent from summary), and whether the reporter had attempted any mitigations. These gaps are acknowledged in information_gaps but were never pursued.

economy.rationale: Both questions were essential and well-targeted. The first efficiently combined search-type characterization with temporal anchoring. The second zoomed in on the fast/slow pattern to find the diagnostic key. No turns were wasted; no information was asked for twice.

question_quality.rationale: The second question was genuinely insightful: rather than asking a checklist of technical questions, it invited the reporter to compare fast versus slow searches from memory, which caused the reporter to independently discover the title-vs-description split — a finding they explicitly say they hadn't connected before. This is exactly the kind of Socratic probing that surfaces information a reporter wouldn't volunteer unprompted.

actionability.rationale: The summary includes a precise root cause hypothesis, step-by-step reproduction instructions, a concrete recommended fix sequence (check v2.3 changelog, verify/rebuild FTS indexes, ensure new fields are indexed before being queried), and a proposed regression test. A developer could open the v2.3 diff and the database schema immediately and know exactly what to look for.

notable_strengths: The second question used comparative memory elicitation ('fast search vs slow search — what was different?') to surface the title/description split without leading the witness — the reporter made the connection themselves
The root cause hypothesis precisely anticipates the actual bug (FTS index regression on description fields) without access to the codebase
The triage summary is developer-ready: reproduction steps, fix sequence, and a regression test case are all concrete and specific
Correctly identified the v2.3 upgrade as the trigger and connected long pasted descriptions to the performance cost
notable_weaknesses: CPU/system resource behavior during slow searches was never asked about, leaving out a key data point that corroborates a full-table-scan hypothesis
OS and hardware details (Ubuntu 22.04, ThinkPad T14, 32GB RAM) were not collected despite the reporter mentioning 'work laptop' — these appear in ground truth but not in the summary
The reporter expressed uncertainty about the version ('I think it was around when I updated to v2.3') but the agent resolved without confirming the exact version
most_insightful_question: Think back to the last time a search was fast and the last time one was slow. What was different about those two moments? — This comparative framing caused the reporter to independently discover the title-vs-description performance split, which is the central diagnostic finding of the entire triage.
missed_opportunities: Ask about CPU or system behavior during a slow search (e.g., 'Does your machine feel sluggish or does a CPU/fan spin up when the slow search runs?') — would have confirmed scan-heavy computation
Confirm the exact TaskFlow version — reporter was uncertain and a one-line follow-up would have locked this down
Ask what OS and hardware the work laptop is running — environment details were available but not collected

Ask whether the reporter had tried any workarounds such as clearing cache, reindexing, or reinstalling — would have revealed that no mitigation was attempted and potentially offered a short-term workaround
